messages.content: --- Iteration 1/5 ---

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Update – Revised Date: March 29

Hey team,

I wanted to give you a heads-up on an important change to our launch timeline.

During testing, we identified a critical bug affecting data sync for enterprise customers. This isn't something we can ship around — it needs to be fixed before we go live, and fixing it right takes time. As a result, we're pushing the launch date from **March 15 to March 29**.

I know this isn't the news anyone wanted to hear, especially after the work we've all put in to get here. That said, shipping something that breaks a core workflow for our enterprise users isn't an option, and two weeks gives us the runway to resolve this properly and re-verify before launch.

Here's what I need from the team right now:

- **[Owner TBD]** to lead the bug investigation and own the fix
- A status update from that workstream by **[date — e.g., end of week]** so we can assess whether March 29 is solid or needs further adjustment
- All other launch-related work to continue on its current track unless you hear otherwise from me

If you have dependencies or deadlines tied to March 15 that we need to revisit, flag them to me directly and we'll work through them.

Thanks for staying focused — let's get this right and launch something we're proud of.

[Your name]
</draft>

---

A few notes on choices I made:

- **Tone** is direct and candid without being alarmist — the reasoning is clear without over-explaining
- The **acknowledgment of effort** is brief; I kept it short so it doesn't feel performative
- The **next steps are action-oriented** with placeholder owners/dates — you'll want to fill those in before sending
- I flagged the **dependency question** proactively, since a date slip usually creates downstream ripple effects worth surfacing early

Want me to adjust the tone, tighten it up, or add anything specific (like a note about external stakeholder communication)?

Solid bones here — the tone is right and it doesn't feel stiff. But the order feels off to me. Right now it leads with the news, then circles back to 'I know this isn't what you wanted to hear' — that acknowledgment should come first, even if it's just one line, before we get into the what and why. Also the closing — 'let's get this right and launch something we're proud of' reads a bit like a pep talk, and I don't think we need that. Once the next step is clear, the email should just end there.

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Update – Revised Date: March 29

Hey team,

I know this isn't the news anyone wanted, but we need to push our launch date.

During testing, we identified a critical bug affecting data sync for enterprise customers. It's not something we can ship around — it needs to be fixed before we go live. As a result, we're moving the launch from **March 15 to March 29**.

Two weeks gives us the runway to resolve this properly and re-verify before launch.

Here's what I need from the team right now:

- **[Owner TBD]** to lead the bug investigation and own the fix
- A status update from that workstream by **[date]** so we can confirm March 29 is solid
- All other launch-related work to continue on its current track unless you hear otherwise from me

If you have dependencies tied to March 15 that we need to revisit, flag them to me directly and we'll work through them.

[Your name]
</draft>

---

Main changes:
- **Moved the acknowledgment to the top** — it's just one line, but it now lands before the news rather than after
- **Cut the closing pep talk entirely** — the email ends on the dependency flag, which is a clean and functional stopping point
- Tightened a couple of lines in the middle while I was in there, nothing structural

Getting closer — the structure is mostly there and the tone is working. The thing that's still nagging me is the 'why' paragraph. 'Two weeks gives us the runway to resolve this properly and re-verify' — that's really about our process, not about what this means for the people reading it. Our engineers have probably already been heads-down prepping for March 15. The reason the date change matters should land from their side of the table, not ours. What does this mean for the work they've already planned around?

Good instinct — that paragraph is doing the wrong job right now. It's justifying the decision to the reader instead of acknowledging what the decision actually does to them.

A few options for how that paragraph could read:

Option 1 (acknowledges disrupted prep work directly):
> For those of you who've been organizing work around March 15, this will mean replanning. I want to make sure that's not happening in the dark — which is why flagging dependencies early matters.

Option 2 (softer, more open-ended acknowledgment):
> I know a lot of you have already been building toward March 15 — that work isn't wasted, but I recognize this shift has real effects on how the next two weeks look for you.

Option 3 (leaner, just names the reality):
> A two-week shift affects plans you've already made, and I don't want anyone replanning in isolation.

All three pivot from "here's why we need the time" to "here's what this means for you" — which is the move you're describing. Option 3 is the most minimal and flows cleanest into the next steps list. Option 1 does the most work to connect the acknowledgment to the dependency flag below it.

Which direction feels right, and I'll slot it in and give you the full draft?

The engineering lead is Marcus — he's the right person to own the dependency check and flag anything that needs to shift. Give him until EOD Thursday (March 6) to surface blockers. Go ahead and fold that into the draft.

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Update – Revised Date: March 29

Hey team,

I know this isn't the news anyone wanted, but we need to push our launch date.

During testing, we identified a critical bug affecting data sync for enterprise customers. It's not something we can ship around — it needs to be fixed before we go live. As a result, we're moving the launch from **March 15 to March 29**.

A two-week shift affects plans you've already made, and I don't want anyone replanning in isolation.

Here's what I need from the team right now:

- **[Owner TBD]** to lead the bug investigation and own the fix
- **Marcus** to surface any dependencies or blockers tied to the date change by **EOD Thursday, March 6** — if the shift affects how your work is planned, get him that information before then
- All other launch-related work to continue on its current track unless you hear otherwise from me

[Your name]
</draft>

---

One thing worth flagging: **[Owner TBD]** is still a placeholder for whoever's leading the bug fix. If that's also Marcus, or if you already know who it is, worth naming them before you send — having an anonymous owner on a critical bug can create ambiguity about who's actually accountable.

This is mostly there — the structure feels right, the tone is landing, and the Marcus ask is clean. The thing that's still not quite working is the action items section. Right now there are three bullets, and two of them don't have a clear owner. '[Owner TBD]' for the bug fix isn't really actionable yet, and 'all other launch-related work continues unless you hear from me' is fine as context but it's not a next step. If the bug fix owner isn't decided yet, either hold that bullet until it is, or fold the intent into the framing so Marcus's ask is the one concrete action the team is being given right now. One owner, one deadline — that's what this section needs.

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Update – Revised Date: March 29

Hey team,

I know this isn't the news anyone wanted, but we need to push our launch date.

During testing, we identified a critical bug affecting data sync for enterprise customers. It's not something we can ship around — it needs to be fixed before we go live. As a result, we're moving the launch from **March 15 to March 29**.

A two-week shift affects plans you've already made, and I don't want anyone replanning in isolation.

For now, the one thing I need from the team: **Marcus is collecting any dependencies or blockers tied to the date change — get him that information by EOD Thursday, March 6.** Everything else continues on its current track until you hear otherwise.

[Your name]
</draft>

---

The action items list is gone — with one owner and one deadline, a list was doing more harm than good. It's now a single clear ask, with the "continue as normal" note folded in as a brief qualifier rather than its own bullet. Cleaner stopping point too.

This is really close — the structure is right, Marcus's ask is clean, and the 'why' is landing from the team's side of the table now. The one small thing: the email ends on 'until you hear otherwise' after the actual action item, which makes it feel like it's still winding down after it should've stopped. The Marcus line is the real close — anything after that dilutes it. Either cut the last sentence or fold that context into the Marcus ask itself so the email ends on the action.

Two ways to handle it:

Option 1 (cut it entirely — Marcus line is the close):
> For now, the one thing I need from the team: **Marcus is collecting any dependencies or blockers tied to the date change — get him that information by EOD Thursday, March 6.**

Option 2 (fold the context into the Marcus line so it ends on the action):
> For now, the one thing I need from the team: **if the shift affects how your work is planned, get that information to Marcus by EOD Thursday, March 6** so nothing gets replanned in isolation.

Option 1 is cleaner — it ends hard on the deadline. Option 2 keeps a little more context but the ending is softer. My instinct is Option 1, since the email has already done the work of explaining why the ask matters.
Option 2 — fold the context in so the email ends on the action. Go ahead and put the full draft together with that closing.

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Update – Revised Date: March 29

Hey team,

I know this isn't the news anyone wanted, but we need to push our launch date.

During testing, we identified a critical bug affecting data sync for enterprise customers. It's not something we can ship around — it needs to be fixed before we go live. As a result, we're moving the launch from **March 15 to March 29**.

A two-week shift affects plans you've already made, and I don't want anyone replanning in isolation.

For now, the one thing I need from the team: **if the shift affects how your work is planned, get that information to Marcus by EOD Thursday, March 6** so nothing gets replanned in isolation.

[Your name]
</draft>

---

One thing to flag: "replanning in isolation" now appears twice — once in the standalone line and once in the closing. Easy fix: cut it from the standalone line and let it land only at the end where it has more weight. Want me to make that adjustment?

This one lands. The structure is exactly right — acknowledgment, news, what it means for the team, one clean ask. The Marcus line does all the work at the end and the email stops there, which is exactly what it needed. Nothing to change.
--- Iteration 2/5 ---

Hey, I need help drafting an email to a key enterprise prospect — they've been in our sales cycle for about three months and just went quiet after we sent the proposal two weeks ago. Our sales rep thinks they might be evaluating a competitor, and we want to re-engage without coming across as desperate or pushy. The goal is to nudge them back into the conversation and ideally get a 30-minute call on the calendar. Want to keep it short — this person is a VP-level buyer and definitely doesn't have time for a wall of text.

<draft>Subject: Quick check-in on where things stand

Hi [Name],

You've put real time into evaluating this — I don't want to let two weeks of silence turn into a dead end if there's still a decision to be made.

If the proposal raised questions, or if priorities have shifted since we last spoke, I'd rather know than guess. A 30-minute call this week or next would let us address whatever's in the way — or at least give you a clear off-ramp if the timing isn't right.

Does Thursday or Friday work for you?</draft>

I like the tone — it doesn't feel desperate, and the CTA is clean. But something feels slightly off about the order of things. It opens with acknowledging the situation, which is right, but then it pivots pretty quickly to what I want out of this ('I don't want to let this turn into a dead end'). That's framing it from my perspective. The reader — a VP who's been quiet for two weeks — probably needs to feel like there's something in this for them before they'll respond. What's the cost to them of staying in limbo? That's what should come before the ask, not my discomfort with the silence.

You're right — that's a rubric issue. The current draft acknowledges the situation but then immediately centers the sender's stake ("I don't want to let this..."), which is exactly what Reader-Centric Framing flags. The cost of limbo to *them* — a decision stalling while their problem stays unsolved — should land before any signal about what the sender wants.

<draft>Subject: Quick check-in on where things stand

Hi [Name],

Two weeks without a decision usually means something's in the way — a competing priority, an unanswered question, or a shift in direction. Any of those is worth a conversation, because the problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't gone away.

If it makes sense to keep talking, I can work around your schedule. If not, a quick note either way lets you close the loop.

Either way — does 30 minutes this week or next work?</draft>

This version is a lot better — the 'problem you were trying to solve in January' line is exactly the right framing, and it doesn't feel pushy at all. Two things are nagging at me though. The opening doesn't quite land as an acknowledgment — it reads more like a diagnosis of their silence, which feels slightly presumptuous for a VP who hasn't asked for my read on why they've gone quiet. And the 'Either way —' before the CTA is doing something weird — it feels like a hedge that softens the actual ask right at the moment I need them to act. The question should just stand on its own.

Both notes are right. "Two weeks without a decision usually means..." is an assumption about their state, not an acknowledgment of it — it puts the sender in the position of explaining the reader's own silence back to them. And "Either way —" is a hedge that cushions the ask right when it should be direct.

<draft>Subject: Quick check-in on where things stand

Hi [Name],

It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal, and I wanted to check in — not to push, but because the problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.

If there are open questions or the priorities have shifted, 30 minutes would be enough to get clarity. Does this week or next work for you?</draft>

This is really close — the framing around their problem is working well, and the CTA is clean. The one thing that's still nagging at me: the opening sentence is doing too much at once. It acknowledges the timeline, but then immediately pivots into the stakes ('a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves') without giving that a moment to breathe. It almost reads like the acknowledgment and the consequence are fighting each other in the same sentence. I want to feel the acknowledgment land first — just one clean beat — and then the stakes follow as their own thought. Right now it's a little compressed. Can you try giving those two ideas a bit more room?

**Rubric Changes have been made:**
- **Reweighted:** "Concrete, Accountable Action Items" (5 → 3)
- **Reweighted:** "Decisive Closure" (3 → 4)
- **Reweighted:** "Economy of Language" (4 → 5)
- **Reworded:** "Emotional Sequencing" description changed
- **Reworded:** "Reader-Centric Framing" description changed
- **Reworded:** "Concrete, Accountable Action Items" description changed
- **Reworded:** "Decisive Closure" description changed
- **Reworded:** "Economy of Language" description changed
- **Reworded:** "Direct, Non-Performative Tone" description changed
- **Dimensions:** "Emotional Sequencing" (+Acknowledgment of effort or situation appears before the news — brief, one sentence max, Order is: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step. No deviations.; -Acknowledgment of impact appears before the difficult news, even if brief, Information unfolds in the order the reader needs to process it, not the order the sender thought of it)
- **Dimensions:** "Reader-Centric Framing" (+Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions from the sender; -Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions or preferences of the sender)
- **Dimensions:** "Concrete, Accountable Action Items" (+Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or narrow timeframe, Action items do not assume org structure details the sender shouldn't know or specify, Each action item has one specific owner — no group asks, no 'everyone please'; -Every action item has a specific, named owner — no '[TBD]' or unnamed accountability, Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or timeframe)
- **Dimensions:** "Decisive Closure" (+No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close — no gratitude, no status reminders, No pep talks, rallying cries, forced optimism, or 'thanks for your patience' at the end; -No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close (status-quo reminders, generic sign-offs), No pep talks, rallying cries, or forced optimism at the end)
- **Dimensions:** "Economy of Language" (+Paragraphs are short — no walls of text, No idea or phrase appears twice — each sentence does unique work; -No idea or phrase appears twice in the email — each sentence does unique work)
- **Dimensions:** "Direct, Non-Performative Tone" (+Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or passive-aggressive softening, No corporate jargon or buzzwords, Avoids motivational phrases, forced optimism, or excessive exclamation marks; -Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or softening, Avoids motivational phrases, forced optimism, or 'we've got this' language)

<draft>Subject: Following up on the proposal

Hi [Name],

It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal.

The problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.

If there are open questions or the priorities have shifted, 30 minutes would get us to clarity. Are you free Thursday or Friday this week?</draft>

*Draft updated based on rubric changes.*

rubric_revision.change_summary: Four rubric changes drove edits here. The updated Emotional Sequencing arc (acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step) prompted restructuring the opening so the news lands explicitly before the reasoning, rather than blending them. The updated Tone criterion's new prohibition on passive-aggressive hedging led to cutting 'not to push.' The updated Action Items criterion's requirement for a specific deadline (not an open range like 'this week or next') prompted replacing the ask with a more concrete anchor. The updated Tone criterion's no-jargon dimension prompted sharpening the subject line away from the softened 'Quick check-in on where things stand.'

annotated_changes.original_text: Quick check-in on where things stand
annotated_changes.reason: Direct, Non-Performative Tone (new dimension 'no_jargon' and updated description adding 'no corporate jargon'): 'Quick check-in on where things stand' is softened, vague workplace filler. 'Following up on the proposal' names the actual subject directly.

annotated_changes.original_text: It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal, and I wanted to check in — not to push, but because the problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.
annotated_changes.new_text:
It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal.

The problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.
annotated_changes.reason: Emotional Sequencing (description and dimension updated to strict arc: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step): The original blended the acknowledgment and reasoning into one run-on sentence. Splitting them gives the news its own beat (the proposal has been sitting for two weeks) before the 'why it matters' reasoning lands. Also: Direct, Non-Performative Tone (dimension 'confident_delivery' updated to include passive-aggressive softening): 'not to push' is a hedge that reads passive-aggressive and was cut.

annotated_changes.original_text: Does this week or next work for you?
annotated_changes.new_text: Are you free Thursday or Friday this week?
annotated_changes.reason: Concrete, Accountable Action Items (priority increased from #5 to #3; dimension 'explicit_deadlines' updated to require specific date or narrow timeframe): 'This week or next' is an open-ended range spanning two weeks. 'Thursday or Friday this week' anchors the ask to a narrow, concrete window as the updated criterion requires.

rubric_revision.original_draft:
Subject: Quick check-in on where things stand

Hi [Name],

It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal, and I wanted to check in — not to push, but because the problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.

If there are open questions or the priorities have shifted, 30 minutes would be enough to get clarity. Does this week or next work for you?

old_rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
old_rubric.description: Information is ordered by how it needs to land on the reader. Acknowledgment of impact comes before the news itself, and the email unfolds context → news → implications → action without circling back to emotional ground already covered.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of impact appears before the difficult news, even if brief
Information unfolds in the order the reader needs to process it, not the order the sender thought of it
The email does not circle back to acknowledge feelings after already delivering the news
The reader knows the general shape of what's coming before specifics land

old_rubric.name: Reader-Centric Framing
old_rubric.description: Impact and reasoning are framed from the audience's side of the table — what this means for their work, plans, and priorities. Every element, including the offer, the options, and any off-ramp, is named as belonging to the reader's decision, not the sender's generosity or anxiety. Avoids sender-side process justifications and avoids language that centers the sender's uncertainty, preference, or stake in the outcome.

dimensions.label: The 'why this matters' is framed from the audience's point of view, not the sender's operational logic
Recognizes work the audience has already done or plans they have already made
Impact is described in terms of concrete effects on the reader's work, not abstract reasoning
Avoids justifying decisions through internal process language ('this gives us the runway to...')
Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions or preferences of the sender

old_rubric.name: Decisive Closure
old_rubric.description: The email ends the moment its work is done. The final substantive line is the call to action — nothing trails after it. Necessary context is folded into the action line rather than appended as a separate sentence.

dimensions.label: The final substantive line is the key ask or call to action
No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close (status-quo reminders, generic sign-offs)
Supporting context is integrated into the action line rather than split into a separate trailing sentence
No pep talks, rallying cries, or forced optimism at the end

old_rubric.name: Economy of Language
old_rubric.description: Every sentence does distinct work. No redundant phrases, repeated ideas, or sections that exist for padding. When a single clear sentence can replace a bullet list, it should. Format follows function — structure is chosen because it serves the content, not convention.

dimensions.label: No idea or phrase appears twice in the email — each sentence does unique work
Structural choices (bullets, paragraphs, headers) are used because they serve the content, not by default
No sections exist solely for convention — every block advances the email's purpose

old_rubric.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
old_rubric.description: Every ask has a named owner and a specific deadline. If an action item can't be made concrete yet, it's held back rather than included as a placeholder. Context is clearly distinguished from asks — background information does not masquerade as a next step. In external-facing contexts where the recipient is not named, specificity means anchoring the ask to a concrete day or narrow window rather than open-ended ranges like 'this week or next.'

dimensions.label: Every action item has a specific, named owner — no '[TBD]' or unnamed accountability
Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or timeframe
Action items are distinguishable from background context — status-quo statements are not framed as asks
Nothing is included as an action item unless it is fully actionable right now

old_rubric.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
old_rubric.description: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. Empathy is genuine but brief — not drawn out or performative. Decisions are delivered with confidence, without excessive hedging or cheerleading.

dimensions.label: Language reads as natural workplace communication — not template-like or overly formal
Empathy and acknowledgment are present but concise — not extended or performative
Avoids motivational phrases, forced optimism, or 'we've got this' language
Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or softening

new_rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
new_rubric.description: Information follows a strict arc: acknowledge effort or situation first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then one concrete next step. Violating this order feels wrong even if all elements are present. The arc should not loop back — once you've moved to news, you don't return to acknowledgment.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of effort or situation appears before the news — brief, one sentence max
Order is: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step. No deviations.
The email does not circle back to acknowledge feelings after already delivering the news
The reader knows the general shape of what's coming before specifics land

new_rubric.name: Reader-Centric Framing
new_rubric.description: The 'why this matters' is always framed from the reader's side — their plans, their team, their work. Never from the sender's internal reasoning, process, or preferences. 'This matters because your team planned around this date' is right. 'This matters because we want to ensure quality' is wrong. Sender-centered justifications feel tone-deaf regardless of intent.

dimensions.label: The 'why this matters' is framed from the audience's point of view, not the sender's operational logic
Recognizes work the audience has already done or plans they have already made
Impact is described in terms of concrete effects on the reader's work, not abstract reasoning
Avoids justifying decisions through internal process language ('this gives us the runway to...')
Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions from the sender

new_rubric.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
new_rubric.description: Every ask has one clear owner and a specific deadline. Group asks like 'everyone please review' are non-actions and should be replaced with a single named owner. At the same time, action items should not over-specify org structure details the email has no business assuming — keep ownership clear without being presumptuous about internal roles or reporting lines.

dimensions.label: Each action item has one specific owner — no group asks, no 'everyone please'
Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or narrow timeframe
Action items are distinguishable from background context — status-quo statements are not framed as asks
Nothing is included as an action item unless it is fully actionable right now
Action items do not assume org structure details the sender shouldn't know or specify

new_rubric.name: Decisive Closure
new_rubric.description: The email ends the moment its work is done. The final substantive line is the call to action — nothing trails after it. No 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational send-offs, no sentiment after the ask. Trailing lines after the CTA weaken the close.

dimensions.label: The final substantive line is the key ask or call to action
No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close — no gratitude, no status reminders
No pep talks, rallying cries, forced optimism, or 'thanks for your patience' at the end
Supporting context is integrated into the action line rather than split into a separate trailing sentence

new_rubric.name: Economy of Language
new_rubric.description: Every sentence does distinct work. No redundant phrases, repeated ideas, or sections that exist for padding. Short paragraphs. Format follows function — structure is chosen because it serves the content, not convention.

dimensions.label: No idea or phrase appears twice — each sentence does unique work
Paragraphs are short — no walls of text
Structural choices (bullets, paragraphs, headers) are used because they serve the content, not by default
No sections exist solely for convention — every block advances the email's purpose

new_rubric.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
new_rubric.description: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. No corporate jargon, no passive-aggressive hedging, no excessive exclamation marks. Empathy is genuine but brief. Decisions are delivered with confidence.

dimensions.label: Language reads as natural workplace communication — not template-like or overly formal
No corporate jargon or buzzwords
Empathy and acknowledgment are present but concise — not extended or performative
Avoids motivational phrases, forced optimism, or excessive exclamation marks
Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or passive-aggressive softening

messages.content:
Here's my feedback on the draft edits:
- Edit "It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal, and I wanted to check in — not to push, but " → "It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal.

The problem you were trying to solve in Jan": Cutting 'not to push' is right — that hedge is passive-aggressive and I hate it. But the replacement cuts off mid-sentence and I have no idea where it was going. 'The problem you were trying to solve in Jan' is a fragment. If the intent was to frame the why from the reader's perspective (which I like), it needs to actually complete the thought. This edit broke the email more than it fixed it.
- Edit "Does this week or next work for you?" → "Are you free Thursday or Friday this week?": I get why they tightened the window, but 'Thursday or Friday this week' is too presumptuous — I don't know their calendar and it comes across as demanding. Something like 'Do you have 20 minutes this week?' is specific enough without boxing them in. The original was too loose, but this overcorrected.
I'd like to revert these changes — let's go back to the original draft and rubric.

<draft>Subject: Quick check-in on where things stand

Hi [Name],

It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal, and I wanted to check in — not to push, but because the problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.

If there are open questions or the priorities have shifted, 30 minutes would be enough to get clarity. Does this week or next work for you?</draft>

*Reverted to original draft and rubric.*
[Error generating response]
I think there was a system hiccup — I didn't actually see a question come through. Can you resend what you were asking? Happy to keep moving on the draft.

No hiccup on my end — I didn't actually have a question, the probe signal tag was a system artifact that shouldn't have been visible. We're good to keep going.

To recap where we landed: the draft above is the agreed-upon version, with two open feedback items still to address:

1. The opening sentence is doing too much — the acknowledgment and the stakes are compressed into one sentence and need to breathe as separate thoughts
2. The CTA window ("this week or next") is too loose, but "Thursday or Friday" overcorrects — you want something specific enough without presuming their calendar

Want me to take another pass with those two fixes?

The prospect's name is Marcus Webb, and the call window I'm targeting is Thursday the 15th or Monday the 19th. Go ahead and plug those in and fix the two open issues — separate the acknowledgment from the stakes, and tighten the CTA to those specific days.

<draft>Subject: Quick check-in on where things stand

Hi Marcus,

It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal.

The problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.

If there are open questions or the priorities have shifted, 30 minutes would get us to clarity. Does Thursday the 15th or Monday the 19th work for you?</draft>

This one's done. The separation of the acknowledgment from the stakes is exactly right — letting 'It's been a couple of weeks' sit on its own before the consequence lands gives it the weight it needed. And 'The problem you were trying to solve in January' keeps the whole thing anchored to Marcus, not to us. The CTA is clean and specific. Send it.
--- Iteration 3/5 ---

I need to send an email to a vendor we've been working with for about six months — they handle our data infrastructure and we're planning to migrate off their platform to a competitor. We haven't told them yet, and the relationship has been decent, so I want to be respectful without being vague or dragging it out. The tricky part is we need their cooperation during the transition period — probably 60 days — so I can't just send a cold "we're leaving" message. Can you help me draft something that's honest, keeps things professional, and sets up that transition conversation?

<draft>Subject: Transition Notice — and a Request for Your Help

Hi [Name],

Working with your team over the past six months has been straightforward, and I've appreciated the responsiveness when issues came up.

We've made the decision to migrate our data infrastructure to a different platform. Our target cutover is 60 days out.

That window matters for your planning — we'll need continued access, documentation support, and likely a few handoff calls before we're fully off your systems.

Can you let me know your availability this week to schedule a transition kickoff call? I'd like to have that conversation before [specific date].

[Your name]</draft>

This is pretty close to what I was looking for — the structure feels right and the tone isn't stiff or apologetic, which I appreciate. The one thing that's nagging at me is that middle paragraph. It says 'that window matters for your planning' but then immediately pivots to listing what *we* need. It ends up reading like our needs are the reason they should care, rather than actually speaking to their side of it — like, what does this mean for how they'll need to resource or prepare for the handoff? I want that paragraph to feel like it's written for them, not just addressed to them.

Good catch — the paragraph is doing reader-centric framing in name only. Here's a revised version of that section:

Option 1 (focused on their resourcing):
"A 60-day window means your team will need to plan for active support through [specific end date] — documentation, access management, and likely a few coordination calls on their side before we're clear."

Option 2 (focused on what they'll be asked to provide):
"Over the next 60 days, we'll be making requests of your team — documentation, access continuity, and handoff support. I want to flag that early so you can resource it appropriately rather than have it land without warning."

Option 3 (leans into mutual clarity):
"For your team, that means roughly 60 days of active transition support — documentation, access management, and coordination calls — before we're fully off your systems. I'd rather give you that lead time than compress it."

Option 2 is probably the strongest — it names the burden directly and frames the early notice as a gesture toward their planning, not just our timeline. But Option 3 works if you want it to feel slightly warmer.

Want me to slot one of these in and give you a full revised draft?

Option 2 feels closer — I want it to be concrete about what we're asking of them, not just vague about "their side." Their main contact on this is probably Marcus, so it would help to name an owner. Can you incorporate Option 2's framing into the full draft and make sure the CTA points to Marcus specifically with a deadline — let's say we want to align on the transition plan by end of next week, so Friday the 14th?

<draft>Subject: Transition Notice — and a Request for Your Help

Hi [Name],

Working with your team over the past six months has been straightforward, and I've appreciated the responsiveness when issues came up.

We've made the decision to migrate our data infrastructure to a different platform. Our target cutover is 60 days out.

Over the next 60 days, we'll be making requests of your team — documentation, access continuity, and handoff support. I want to flag that early so Marcus can resource it appropriately rather than have it land without warning.

Can you connect me with Marcus directly so we can align on a transition plan by Friday the 14th?

[Your name]</draft>

This is really close — the structure is clean, the CTA is specific, and it doesn't drag. The one thing I keep circling back to: that middle paragraph still centers our thoughtfulness a bit too much. 'So Marcus can resource it appropriately rather than have it land without warning' is framed around us being considerate, but what I really want is something that speaks to what's actually at stake for their team — like, if they're not looped in early, they're the ones scrambling to pull docs and manage access on a compressed timeline. Can you reframe that sentence so it's less about us flagging early and more about what it means for their team if they're not prepared?

Here are a few options for that sentence only:

Option 1 (consequence-forward):
"If Marcus isn't looped in early, his team will be pulling documentation and managing access on a compressed timeline — which makes the handoff harder for everyone, but the scramble lands on their side."

Option 2 (cleaner, stakes-first):
"Without early coordination, Marcus's team ends up managing documentation, access, and handoff requests under time pressure — and that's a difficult position to be in at the back end of a 60-day window."

Option 3 (most direct):
"That's a real ask of Marcus's team, and if they're not resourced for it from the start, the back half of that window gets difficult fast."

Option 2 is probably the strongest fit — it names the specific burden (documentation, access, handoff), ties it to the timeline, and frames the consequence from their side without making us the hero for flagging it early. Option 3 is shortest but loses some of the operational specificity you've been pushing toward.

Want me to slot one in and give you the full draft?
Option 2 is stronger — "difficult position to be in at the back end" is the right framing. Go with that one and put the full draft together with it incorporated.

<draft>Subject: Transition Notice — and a Request for Your Help

Hi [Name],

Working with your team over the past six months has been straightforward, and I've appreciated the responsiveness when issues came up.

We've made the decision to migrate our data infrastructure to a different platform. Our target cutover is 60 days out.

Over the next 60 days, we'll be making requests of your team — documentation, access continuity, and handoff support. Without early coordination, Marcus's team ends up managing those requests under time pressure — and that's a difficult position to be in at the back end of a 60-day window.

Can you connect me with Marcus directly so we can align on a transition plan by Friday the 14th?

[Your name]</draft>

This lands exactly right. The structure is clean, the tone is honest without being cold, and that 'difficult position to be in at the back end of a 60-day window' line does exactly what I was pushing for — it's about them, not us being thoughtful. The CTA is specific and actionable. I'd send this.
--- Iteration 4/5 ---

Hey, I need to write an email to a customer success manager at one of our larger accounts — they've been a strong advocate for us internally, but their company just announced layoffs and we know their team got hit. I want to acknowledge what's happening without being tone-deaf or opportunistic, and also flag that their renewal is coming up in about six weeks. It's a tricky balance — I don't want it to feel like I'm using the layoffs as a leverage point, but I also can't just ignore the renewal entirely. Any help?

<draft>Subject: Checking in — and a heads up on renewal timing

Hi [Name],

I saw the news about the layoffs and wanted to reach out.

Your renewal lands in about six weeks, and depending on how your team structure has shifted, there may be decisions you need to make before then — around scope, seat count, or who's owning the relationship on your end going forward.

I'd rather you have the lead time than be scrambling close to the date. If it's useful, I'm available Tuesday or Wednesday of next week to talk through where things stand.

[Your name]</draft>

The overall tone is good — it doesn't feel opportunistic, which was the hard part. And the closing with specific days is clean. But that middle section feels a little off to me. The line 'I'd rather you have the lead time than be scrambling' — that's framing it from my side. What I actually want is for it to land more like: here's what this means *for you* given everything that's shifting on your end. The stakes should feel like their problem to solve, not my reason for writing. Does that make sense? The 'why this matters' piece needs to be more about what they're navigating, not what I'd prefer.

That makes complete sense — the current line centers your preference, not their situation. The fix is to drop the sender-as-considerate-actor framing and just describe the operational reality they're facing.

<draft>Subject: Checking in — and a heads up on renewal timing

Hi [Name],

I saw the news about the layoffs and wanted to reach out.

Your renewal is six weeks out. With team structure changing, you may need to sort out seat count, scope, or who's carrying the relationship before that date arrives.

If it's useful to talk through where things stand, I'm available Tuesday or Wednesday of next week.

[Your name]</draft>

This version is much closer — the middle paragraph now reads like their problem to solve, not my reason for reaching out, which is exactly what I was going for. The tone feels right throughout.

The one thing that's still a little fuzzy: the ask at the end. 'If it's useful to talk through where things stand' is pretty open-ended — it doesn't really push toward any one person taking action by any particular point. Given that their team just got reshuffled, there's probably some ambiguity on their end about who even owns this. I'd want the closing to do a bit more work there — not over-specify their org structure, but make it clearer what the actual next step is and who should be driving it.

Good catch. The current close is too passive — "if it's useful" hands it back to them without a clear owner or direction. The fix is to name yourself as the one taking the next step, with a specific prompt they can simply respond to.

<draft>Subject: Checking in — and a heads up on renewal timing

Hi [Name],

I saw the news about the layoffs and wanted to reach out.

Your renewal is six weeks out. With team structure changing, you may need to sort out seat count, scope, or who's carrying the relationship before that date arrives.

Does Tuesday or Wednesday of next week work for a call? If someone else on your team should be looped in, feel free to forward this along.

[Your name]</draft>

The structure is working well now — the middle paragraph reads like their situation to manage, not my reason for emailing. And 'Does Tuesday or Wednesday work' is clean and specific.

The last line is the remaining snag. 'Feel free to forward this along' is a bit of a loose end — it's not quite a group ask, but it's also not a clear action for anyone. If there's ambiguity about who owns the relationship on their end, I'd rather acknowledge that directly and put the ball back in one person's court — the person I'm writing to — rather than leave it open with a vague 'pass this along if needed.' Can you take another pass at that closing so it ends on the call ask without trailing off into an optional action?

Agreed — that line dilutes the close. The email is addressed to one person; the CTA should land there and stop.

<draft>Subject: Checking in — and a heads up on renewal timing

Hi [Name],

I saw the news about the layoffs and wanted to reach out.

Your renewal is six weeks out. With team structure changing, you may need to sort out seat count, scope, or who's carrying the relationship before that date arrives.

Does Tuesday or Wednesday of next week work for a call?

[Your name]</draft>
Yeah, this is it. The arc lands right — acknowledgment, their situation, concrete ask. Nothing extra hanging off the end. I'd send this.
--- Iteration 5/5 ---

Hey, I need help drafting an email to a new hire on my team — she's a junior product analyst who's been with us for about six weeks. She's smart and eager, but I've been noticing she's been jumping into Slack threads and customer calls before she has enough context, and it's starting to create some confusion with a couple of our accounts. I want to address it directly without making her feel like she's in trouble or killing her confidence — she's genuinely good and I want her to stick around. It's a tricky balance.

<draft>Subject: Quick Sync — Wanted to Chat About Something

Hi [Name],

Six weeks in and you're already making an impact — genuinely glad you're on the team.

I did want to flag something, and I'm mentioning it because I think it'll help you hit your stride faster. A few times recently, you've jumped into Slack threads or customer calls before having the full picture, and it's caused some confusion on a couple of accounts. Totally understandable at this stage — but worth course-correcting now.

Can we grab 20 minutes this week to talk through how to approach those situations going forward? Just reply with a time that works.

— [Your Name]</draft>

Honestly, the tone is pretty close to what I want — it doesn't feel punishing and the CTA is clean. But the part that explains why this matters is landing a little off for me. Right now it reads like you're telling her why *you're* bringing it up, not why *she* should care. Something like 'this is costing you credibility with the accounts you're trying to build relationships with' would hit differently than 'I think it'll help you hit your stride.' Can you rework that piece so it's about what's actually at stake for her?

Good catch — that line was sender-framed. Here's the rework:

<draft>Subject: Quick Sync — Wanted to Chat About Something

Hi [Name],

Six weeks in and you're already making an impact — genuinely glad you're on the team.

I did want to flag something directly. A few times recently, you've jumped into Slack threads or customer calls before having the full picture, and it's starting to cost you credibility with the accounts you're working to build relationships with. Those early impressions are hard to walk back.

Can we grab 20 minutes this week to talk through how to approach those situations going forward? Just reply with a time that works.

— [Your Name]</draft>

Yeah, this version lands much better. The 'early impressions are hard to walk back' line does a lot of work — it's direct without being harsh, and it's clearly about what's at stake for *her*, not some abstract team concern. The CTA is clean and doesn't overstay its welcome. I'd send this.

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: Information follows a strict arc: acknowledge the situation or the reader first (brief — one sentence max, can be appreciative or sympathetic depending on context), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters to the reader, then one concrete next step. Each beat gets its own sentence or paragraph — compressing two beats into one robs them of weight. The arc never loops back.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of effort, situation, or relationship appears before the news — brief, one sentence max
Each major beat (acknowledgment, news, stakes, ask) occupies its own sentence or paragraph — no two beats compressed into one
The email does not circle back to acknowledge feelings after already delivering the news
The reader knows the general shape of what's coming before specifics land
Order is: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step — no deviations or revisiting earlier beats

rubric.name: Reader-Centric Framing
rubric.description: The 'why this matters' is always framed from the reader's side — their plans, their credibility, their work, their consequences. Never from the sender's reasoning, process, or considerate intent. Framing early notice, transparency, or restraint as a gesture still centers the sender. The test: does this sentence describe a consequence the reader faces, or an action the sender is taking? Only the former passes. This extends to every sentence in the email — scheduling, correction, logistics, and word choice all stay in the reader's frame.

dimensions.label: The stakes or consequences are framed from the audience's point of view — what changes for them, not why the sender made the decision
The stakes paragraph names specific operational implications for the reader — what they will need to do, provide, or manage — not abstract impact
The sender's actions (flagging early, being transparent, giving notice) are not framed as gestures of consideration — consequences are stated as facts about the reader's situation
Avoids justifying decisions through sender-side process language ('this gives us the runway to...', 'we want to ensure quality')
Does not interpret or diagnose the reader's behavior, motivations, or silence — observation is fine, attribution is not
Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions from the sender
Scheduling details and logistical language do not anchor to the sender's availability or preference — they are offered as neutral options for the reader to accept or redirect

rubric.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
rubric.description: Every ask has one clear owner and a specific deadline. If an action item can't be made concrete yet, it's held back rather than included as a placeholder. Context is clearly distinguished from asks. When org structure is unclear, ownership defaults to the addressee rather than being diffused with optional forwards or vague 'loop someone in' language. CTA specificity is calibrated to the relationship — offer specific dates as neutral options without presuming or commanding the reader's schedule.

dimensions.label: Each action item has one specific owner — no '[TBD]', no unnamed accountability, no group asks
Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or narrow timeframe — not a bracket, not 'soon'
Action items are distinguishable from background context — status-quo statements are not framed as asks
Nothing is included as an action item unless it is fully actionable right now — no bracketed stand-ins or deferred specifics
When org structure or ownership is ambiguous, the ask lands with the person being emailed — no diffuse optional forwards or 'pass this along if needed'
CTA offers concrete date options as neutral choices — not so loose as to be non-committal ('sometime soon'), not so narrow as to presume the reader's calendar

rubric.name: Decisive Closure
rubric.description: The email ends the moment its work is done. The final substantive line is the call to action — nothing trails after it. No hedging or softening immediately before the CTA. No gratitude, pep talks, optional actions, vague 'pass this along' language, or trailing sentiment after it. Necessary context is folded into the action line rather than appended as a separate sentence.

dimensions.label: The final substantive line is the key ask or call to action
No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close — no gratitude, no status reminders, no optional follow-ups, no vague 'forward this along' invitations
No hedging or softening language ('either way,' 'no pressure,' 'if you have time') immediately before the ask
Supporting context is integrated into the action line rather than split into a separate trailing sentence
No pep talks, rallying cries, forced optimism, or 'thanks for your patience' at the end

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
rubric.description: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. Empathy is genuine but brief — not drawn out or performative. Decisions are delivered with confidence. No passive-aggressive hedges ('not to push, but...', 'just checking in') — these read as insincere and are considered tone-deaf. No corporate jargon, no cheerleading, no forced optimism.

dimensions.label: Language reads as natural workplace communication — not template-like or overly formal
Empathy and acknowledgment are present but concise — not extended or performative
No passive-aggressive softeners like 'not to push,' 'just checking in,' or 'no pressure but' — these undermine directness and read as insincere
No corporate buzzwords or jargon — plain, direct language throughout
Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or passive-aggressive softening

rubric.name: Economy of Language
rubric.description: Every sentence does distinct work. No redundant phrases, repeated ideas, or sections that exist for padding. Short paragraphs — no walls of text. Format follows function: structure is chosen because it serves the content, not convention. When a single clear sentence can replace a bullet list, it should.

dimensions.label: No idea or phrase appears twice — each sentence does unique work
Paragraphs are short — no dense blocks of text
Structural choices (bullets, paragraphs, single sentences) are used because they serve the content, not by default
No sections exist solely for convention — every block advances the email's purpose

rubric.user_goals_summary: This user writes emails that are structurally clean, reader-centric, and end the moment the work is done. They value a strict emotional arc (acknowledge → news → reader-side stakes → one concrete ask), and they are exceptionally attuned to sender-framed language masquerading as reader empathy. Tone should be direct and conversational without hedging, cheerleading, or performative gestures.
rubric.coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is reader-centric framing — they will catch sender-framed language in every draft, including subtle forms like 'I'm flagging this early so you can plan.' They have a precise internal sense of emotional sequencing and will feel when two beats are compressed or out of order even before they can articulate why. Their instinct is always to tighten and cut: pep talks, trailing sentiment, vague optional actions, and placeholder asks are all reliably rejected. When offering options, present 2–3 concrete choices and let them select rather than asking open-ended questions.
